coredata.dc:title: A facile synthesis of water-soluble copper nanoclusters as label-free fluorescent probes for rapid, selective and sensitive determination of alizarin red
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Zhifeng
Zhang, Yixuan
Zhao, Manlin
Bao, Jinjia
Lv, Ling
Li, Haoyang
coredata.dc:description: Copper nanoclusters (FA@CuNCs) emitting blue fluorescence were successfully developed via a one-pot technique. In this method, the copper chloride, folic acid and hydrazine hydrate were applied as a precursor, protective agent and reducing agent, respectively. The absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of FA@CuNCs were carried out by using ultraviolet–visible and fluorescence spectrometry, respectively. The morphology, particle size, functional groups, oxidation states of elements of FA@CuNCs were discussed via using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The stability of FA@CuNCs was studied under various conditions, such as storage time at 25 ℃, ultraviolet radiation time, sodium chloride solutione and pH. The FA@CuNCs displayed blue fluorescence under the excitation wavelength of 361 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yield was 7.45 %. As a result of the inner filter effect, the alizarin red could significantly weaken the blue fluorescence of FA@CuNCs. Thus, the as-prepared FA@CuNCs could be utilized as fluorescence nanosensors for the trace determination of alizarin red. This platform suggested an excellent linear range for alizarin red varying from 0.5 to 200 μM with a fitting coefficient of 0.9955. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.064 μM in the light of the 3b/k (b and k refer to the standard deviation and slope of fitted curve, respectively). Furthermore, the as-developed FA@CuNCs could be used to detect the alizarin red in real samples and for the sensing of temperature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Alizarin red determination
Inner filter effect
Temperature sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of alizarin red in real samples
Measurement of alizarin red and quenching mechanism
Measurement of alizarin red in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank the College Students\u2019 Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2221) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The full XPS spectrum (A), C1s single XPS spectrum (B), N1s single XPS spectrum (C), O1s single XPS spectrum (D), Cu2p single XPS spectrum (E) and TEM image (F) of FA@CuNCs.
The UV–vis, excitation and emission spectra of FA@CuNCs (A); the emission spectra under different excitation wavelength (changing from 340 nm to 380 nm) (B).

Fluorescence response of folic acid capped copper nanoclusters (FA@CuNCs) towards 0–200 μM alizarin red (A); the linear plot of the ln(F0/F) of FA@CuNCs at 445 nm against the concentration of alizarin red (0.5–200 μM) (B). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The UV–vis spectrum of alizarin red, excitation (λem = 445 nm) and emission (λex = 361 nm) spectra of FA@CuNCs (A); the fluorescence lifetime of FA@CuNCs and FA@CuNCs + alizarin red (B); the linear relationship between F0/F and different concentrations of alizarin red (0.5–100 μM) (C). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence response of folic acid capped copper nanoclusters (FA@CuNCs) towards 15–65 ℃ temperature (A); the relationship between fluorescence intensity and different temperature (15–65 ℃) (B); the cycle curve of FA@CuNCs between 15 ℃ and 65 ℃ (C).
Comparison of different analytical methods for alizarin red determination.
The alizarin red detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: A facile synthesis of water-soluble copper nanoclusters as label-free fluorescent probes for rapid, selective and sensitive determination of alizarin red

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong, 030619, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong, 030619, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper nanoclusters (FA@CuNCs) emitting blue fluorescence were successfully developed via a one-pot technique. In this method, the copper chloride, folic acid and hydrazine hydrate were applied as a precursor, protective agent and reducing agent, respectively. The absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of FA@CuNCs were carried out by using ultraviolet–visible and fluorescence spectrometry, respectively. The morphology, particle size, functional groups, oxidation states of elements of FA@CuNCs were discussed via using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The stability of FA@CuNCs was studied under various conditions, such as storage time at 25 ℃, ultraviolet radiation time, sodium chloride solutione and pH. The FA@CuNCs displayed blue fluorescence under the excitation wavelength of 361 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yield was 7.45 %. As a result of the inner filter effect, the alizarin red could significantly weaken the blue fluorescence of FA@CuNCs. Thus, the as-prepared FA@CuNCs could be utilized as fluorescence nanosensors for the trace determination of alizarin red. This platform suggested an excellent linear range for alizarin red varying from 0.5 to 200 μM with a fitting coefficient of 0.9955. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.064 μM in the light of the 3b/k (b and k refer to the standard deviation and slope of fitted curve, respectively). Furthermore, the as-developed FA@CuNCs could be used to detect the alizarin red in real samples and for the sensing of temperature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Alizarin red (Fig. S1), as an anthraquinone derivatives, is commonly used to detect calcium changes in tissue slices or cultured cells [1]. Its mechanism of action is the chelation of alizarin red with calcium to form an orange red complex [2–5]. In addition, alizarin red is also an acid-base indicator and its color range is between pH 3.7 (yellow) and 5.2 (purple) [6,7]. Moreover, alizarin red is one of the common organic pollutants. To today, the detection method of alizarin red have only fluorescence method [8,9]. As far as we know, the fluorescence strategy has carried out huge interest due to its excellent selectivity and simple experiment process [10,11].

Compared with organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots and metal organic skeleton structure, metal nanoclusters possess some advantages, including fascinating biocompatibility, excellent photostability, large Stokes shift, mild development conditions (lower temperature, time-saving and water-solubility).These advantages provide more guarantees for its applications in various aspects, such as fluorescence determination, cell imaging, temperature sensing and catalysis [12–18]. Generally, many ligands containing active functional groups can be applied for the development of metal nanoclusters [19]. On the one hand, these ligands can play an important role in protecting metal nanoclusters [20]. On the other hand, these ligands can also improve the optical properties and structure of metal nanoclusters [21]. To today, these protective agents mainly include amino acids, proteins, DNA, polymers and thiol compounds (histidine, folic acid, tryptophan and 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid) [22–29]. Because the one-pot strategy is a facile, rapid and economic development procedure. Thus, the most commonly used method in preparation strategies is the one-pot method.

As far as we know, the metal nanoclusters include gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) and copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) [30–33]. Compared with AuNCs and AgNCs, CuNCs also display fascinating fluorescence features, even have significant price advantage. However, the biggest problem for CuNCs is that Cu0 is easily oxidized to Cu2+. Therefore, the specific operation process and protectant have become a bit difficult. For example, Yang et al. developed L-cystine modified CuNCs by using ascorbic acid as a reducing agent [34]. However, the as-prepared CuNCs suggested very weak fluorescence intensity. Zhao et al. [35] prepared CuNCs via using penicilamine as a protectant and reducing agent, and this CuNCs held strong fluorescence. However, the water solubility of CuNCs was relatively poor. Thus, it was necessary to develop CuNCs with considerable stability, water solubility and biocompatibility. As far as we know, folic acid had many functional groups (carbonyl, hydroxyl and amino groups), which was an important guarantee for CuNCs with good stability and water solubility [36,37]. Thus, folic acid could be developed as an appropriate protecting ligand for the preparation of CuNCs.

In this paper, FA@CuNCs were carried out by utilizing copper chloride as a precursor, folic acid as a capping ligand and hydrazine hydrate as a reducing agent. The absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of FA@CuNCs were carried out by using ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectrometry, respectively. The morphology, particle size, functional groups, oxidation states of elements of FA@CuNCs were discussed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Meanwhile, we developed a quick, facile and practical measurement method for alizarin red based on the FA@CuNCs. The fluorescence detection mechanism was ascribed to the inner filter effect (IFE). Furthermore, this nanoprobe could be successfully used for the alizarin red sensing in bovine serum and river water samples. At last, the as-prepared FA@CuNCs could also be applied for the sensing of temperature.

Copper chloride dihydrate (AR), folic acid (98 %), hydrazine hydrate (80 %), sodium chloride (99.5 %), potassium chloride (99.5 %), calcium chloride dihydrate (AR), sodium bicarbonate (≥99.8 %), sodium sulfate (99 %), sodium sulfite (98 %), sodium bisulfite (AR), glycine (≥99 %), alanine (99 %), glucose monohydrate (98 %), glutamic acid (99 %), arginine (98 %), tryptophan (99 %), proline (99 %), glutathione (98 %), sucrose (AR), fructose (99 %), tartrazine (>95 %), sunset yellow (87 %) congo red (AR) and alizarin red (97 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All materials were directly used for detection tests without any purification. All used solutions were configured by using ultrapure water.

The fluorescence excitation and emission data were recorded on an F-7000 fluorescence instrument (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), and the excitation/emission slit widths and the scanning speed were 10 nm/5 nm and 1200 nm/mir, respectively. The absorption spectra of FA@CuNCs, alizarin red and FA@CuNCs + alizarin red mixture were obtained on a UV-4500 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), and the scanning range and speed were set as 200–800 nm and 1200 nm/mir. The morphology and size of FA@CuNCs were discussed by using a Tecnai20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI, USA). The oxidation state of all elements were studied via using an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, USA). The types of functional group were carried out on a Nicolet 370 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific, USA), and the scanning range was developed as 4000–400 cm−1. An FLS 980 steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) was utilized to measure the fluorescence lifetime of FA@CuNCs without and with addition of alizarin red. A PHS-3E pH meter (Shanghai, China) was used to record the pH value of solutions.

The FA@CuNCs were developed via using a one-pot strategy (folic acid as a protective agent and hydrazine hydrate as a reducing agent) [38]. First of all, the copper chloride solution (6 mL, 0.05 M) and folic acid (0.176 g) were added into 100 mL beaker, and the mixture was stirred with thermostatic magnetic stirrer for 10 min at room temperature. Soon afterwards, the hydrazine hydrate (100 μL) was continuously dropped into the mixture solution and the pH value was adjusted to 6. Then the solution was reacted for 15 h at 40 ℃. The blue solution was obtained under UV light, indicating the successful preparation of FA@CuNCs. At last, the product was purified with a dialysis membrane (MW: 3500 Da) for 24 h at room temperature and stored at 4 ℃ for further use.

First of all, a sensing platform was developed based on the FA@CuNCs and phosphate buffered saline solution (PBS, pH = 6, synthesized via KH2PO4, Na2HPO4, NaCl and KCl solution [39]). Subsequently, various concentrations of alizarin red were introduced into the designed sensing platform. After the incubation of 2 min, the mixture was added into the fluorescent cuvette (1 cm × 1 cm). At the same time, the fluorescence spectra and intensities were obtained on an F-7000 fluorescence instrument with the excitation wavelength of 361 nm. Then, the corresponding linear range and detection limit (LOD) were obtained on the basis of the above corresponding data. For the selective experiments, various reference materials were introduced separately into the parallel sensing solutions. The reference materials had Na+, K+, Ca2+, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, HSO3 -, glycine, alanine, glucose monohydrate, glutamic acid, arginine, tryptophan, proline, glutathione, sucrose, fructose, tartrazine, sunset yellow and congo red (the concentration was 200 μM). The fluorescence intensities were recorded under the same excitation wavelength and excitation/emission slits.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of alizarin red in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The measurement of alizarin red were studied in river water and bovine serum samples. The river water samples were obtained from Fenhe in Shanxi Province, China. Before detection of alizarin red, the river water samples were filtrated and centrifuged successively with qualitative filter and centrifuge (10000 r/min for 20 min). The bovine serum samples were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Then, the bovine serum samples were diluted 20 times with PBS solution (pH = 6). In detail, the standard alizarin red solutions were added into the treated real samples, and the final concentrations of alizarin red were 30 and 50 μM, respectively. Then the fluorescence data were obtained via the F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The recoveries were carried out through the linear fitting equation.

The fluorescence quantum yield of FA@CuNCs was carried out on the basis of the strategy reported in the published references [40]. The quinine sulfate was used as a reference. The absorbance of FA@CuNCs and quinine sulfate were performed on the ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra of FA@CuNCs and quinine sulfate were carried out via using the fluorescence spectrophotometer. The specific calculation formula was displayed as follows.
QY = Q
Y R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2
where QY and QYR represent the fluorescent quantum yields of the FA@CuNCs and quinine sulfate; I and I R refer to the integral areas of fluorescence spectrum for the FA@CuNCs and quinine sulfate; A and AR represent the absorbance of the FA@CuNCs and quinine sulfate under the excitation wavelength of 361 nm; η and η R refer to the refractive index of the FA@CuNCs and quinine sulfate. The fluorescence quantum yield was 7.45 %. Compared with some reported Cu NCs, the quantum yield suggested significant advantages [41,42].

FT-IR, as one of the four major spectra, is an important measurement method for the identification of functional groups. The FT-IR spectrum of FA@CuNCs was suggested in Fig. S2. From Fig. S2, the characteristic peaks at 3385.9 cm−1 and 1633.8 cm−1 brought about by the symmetric stretching of OH and C = O bonds, respectively [43]. The asymmetric stretching vibration peak of C-O bond was located at 1145.9 cm−1 [44]. The deformation vibration absorption peaks of C–H and O–H bonds appeared at 1359.2 cm−1 and 1092.5 cm−1, respectively. The peak at 982.1 cm−1 was attributed to the characteristic peak of the OH…O = bond [45]. The binding energy of C1s, N1s, O1s and Cu2p were 285.6, 400.2, 531.4 and 934.8 eV in full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1 A) [46]. At the same time, the single XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s and Cu2p were subjected to peak splitting processing for the study of the element oxidation state. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the original spectrogram of C1s was divided into three peaks at 284.5, 285.6 and 287.6 eV, which were attributed to C–C, C-N and O-C = O bonds, respectively [47]. The peaks at 398.0 and 400.6 eV were ascribed to the C-N and N–H bonds (Fig. 1C) [48]. In single XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1D), two peaks at 530.3 and 531.8 eV were respectively assigned to O–H and C = O bonds, respectively. As suggested in Fig. 1E, two peaks at 931.7 and 952.4 eV were ascribed to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 ionization of Cu+ or Cu0 [49]. The peak at 942 eV was not discovered, suggesting the absence of Cu2+. Furthermore, the presence of Cu+ made a significant contribution to the fluorescence intensity and stability of the FA@CuNCs [50]. The TEM image of FA@CuNCs was displayed in Fig. 1F. As we could see from Fig. 1F, the FA@CuNCs suggested spherical particles in aqueous solution. In Fig. S3, the average particle size was 2.7 ± 0.04 nm, which was equivalent to that of reported Cu NCs [51,52]. These characterization results displayed that the FA@CuNCs were successfully developed via using the above one-pot strategy.

The optical features of FA@CuNCs were discussed by using UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer. First, the color of the FA@CuNCs solution was observed. In Fig. 2 A, the FA@CuNCs solution suggested colorless and blue under sunlight and ultraviolet light (365 nm), respectively. Meanwhile, the excitation and emission spectra were also displayed in Fig. 2A. The optimum emission wavelength was 445 nm under the excitation wavelength of 361 nm. In UV–vis absorption spectrum, a noticeable peak at 264 nm was ascribed to the energy level transition. Besides, as suggested in Fig. 2B, the emission wavelength of FA@CuNCs appeared a certain change with the excitation wavelength changing from 340 nm to 380 nm, implying that the FA@CuNCs held an excitation-dependent performance [53]. Next, the stability of FA@CuNCs was studied under various conditions, such as storage time at 25 ℃, ultraviolet radiation time, sodium chloride solutione and pH. In Fig. S4A, the FA@CuNCs displayed surprising stability at 25 ℃. After 3.5 min of irradiation, the fluorescence suggested a slight decrease but could be ignored, exhibiting the excellent photostability of FA@CuNCs (Fig. S4B). When the NaCl concentration reached 0.08 M in FA@CuNCs solution, there was a slight increase in fluorescence, indicating the significant tolerance in salt solution (Fig. S4C). As displayed in Fig. S4D, the fluorescence intensity would significantly decrease under alkaline conditions. The excellent stability of FA@CuNCs was beneficial for wider applications in many fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of alizarin red and quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime of FA@CuNCs and alizarin red were discussed. In Fig. 4A, two noticeable absorption peak at 334 and 521 nm were performed, and the excitation and emission wavelength were 361 and 445 nm. It was thus clear that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of alizarin red overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of FA@CuNCs. Thus, the fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or fluorescence inner filter effect (IFE) [54]. To our knowledge, the fluorescence lifetime could distinguish the FRET and IFE [55]. From fluorescence lifetime spectra (Fig. 4B), the fluorescence lifetime value of FA@CuNCs without and with the addition of alizarin red were 10.04 ns and 9.97 ns. The slight difference could exclude the possibility of FRET. Thus, the quenching mechanism was IFE. Subsequently, it was necessary to discuss the possibility of static quenching or dynamic quenching. We used the Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = 1 + K SV[Q] = 1 + k q τ 0[Q]) to perform the above quenching [56]. In equation, F0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensity of FA@CuNCs in the absence and presence of alizarin red. K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. The alizarin red concentration is marked as [Q]. τ 0 = 10.04 ns. Based on the Stern-Volmer equation and linear fitting curve (Fig. 4 C), the K SV and k q were calculated to be 1.0 × 104 L·mol−1 and k q value of 1.0 × 1012 L·mol−1·s−1 (>2 × 1010 L·mol−1·s−1), suggesting that the other mechanism was static quenching [57].

From Fig. S6, the effect of control materials on the F0/F value of the detection system was studied. The control materials had Na+, K+, Ca2+, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, HSO3 -, glycine, alanine, glucose, monohydrate, glutamic acid, arginine, tryptophan, proline, glutathione, sucrose and fructose. These control materials displayed tenuous fluorescence quenching influence on the detection system. However, alizarin red, tartrazine, sunset yellow and congo red exhibited strong fluorescence quenching effect. Furthermore, the interference tests were studied on the basis of selectivity experiments with the addition of alizarin red, indicating the little influence on the measurement of alizarin red (except for tartrazine, sunset yellow and congo red) (Fig. S7). Thus, except for similar substances (tartrazine, sunset yellow and congo red), FA@CuNCs displayed certain selectivity for the detection of alizarin red.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of alizarin red in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the actual application, the FA@CuNCs were performed as detection systems for the sensing of alizarin red in river water and bovine serum samples. The detected concentrations, recovery rate and relative standard deviation (RSD) in real samples were implied in Table 2 . As suggested in Table 2, the recovery rate of 94.7–107.3 % in river water sample and that of 95.3–95.7 % in bovine serum sample were obtained. The corresponding RSD value ranges were 2.55–3.07 % and 2.69–2.93 %, respectively. The analysis results suggested that the as-performed FA@CuNCs were credible sensors that could be exploited for the alizarin red sensing in actual samples.

As suggested in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence emission spectra of measurement system were carried out at different temperature. The fluorescence intensity appeared a weakening phenomenon with increasing temperature from 15 to 65 ℃. In addition, we discussed the linear detection of temperature. The relationship between F and temperature was investigated. As we can see from Fig. 5B, a fascinating linear curve was developed between F and temperature in the range of 15–65 ℃. The linear equation and fitting coefficient were F = -5.96Tem + 729 and 0.9750, respectively. Amazingly, this sensing platform could be cycled several times between 15 ℃ and 65 ℃, and its fluorescence intensity had no significant difference from the initial value (Fig. 5 C).

Herein, we designed a novel fluorescence nanosensor in the light of FA@CuNCs for sensitive sensing of alizarin red through a one-pot preparation method. The as-performed nanosensor emerged amazing stability under different conditions. At the same time, this sensor could effectively determine alizarin red accompanying a fascinating linear relationship and a lower LOD. The fluorescence quenching mechanisms were ascribed to the IFE and static quenching. Soon afterwards, this sensor was also successfully used for the measurement of alizarin red in Fenhe river water and bovine serum samples, which held excellent recovery rates and reliable RSD values. Furthermore, the as-prepared FA@CuNCs could be applied as temperature sensors for the sensing of temperature.

Zhifeng Cai: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Yixuan Zhang: Supervision, Software. Manlin Zhao: Supervision, Software. Jinjia Bao: Software. Ling Lv: Supervision, Software. Haoyang Li: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2221) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124708.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

J. Photochem. Photobio. A
